did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel AI method called Anti Coronavirus Optimized Kernel-based Softplus Extreme Learning Machine (ACO-KSELM) for the classification of skin cancer. It applies this machine learning model to dermoscopic image datasets (ISIC 2016, ACS, HAM10000, PAD-UFES-20) to classify lesions into categories such as Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Nevus.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel algorithm (ACO-KSELM) and presents original experimental results, including specific accuracy metrics (98.9%, 98.7%, 98.6%, 97.9%) on four distinct datasets (ISIC 2016, ACS, HAM10000, PAD-UFES-20), indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: ACO-KELM: Anti Coronavirus Optimized Kernel-based Softplus Extreme Learning Machine for classification of skin cancer
brief_summary: This study proposes the Anti Coronavirus Optimized Kernel-based Softplus Extreme Learning Machine (ACO-KSELM) to address the challenge of redundant features in high-dimensional biomedical datasets for skin cancer classification. The method utilizes four dermoscopic image datasets: ISIC 2016, ACS, HAM10000, and PAD-UFES-20. Preprocessing involved Gaussian filtering, followed by feature extraction using color histograms, Haralick texture, and Hu moments. The model classifies lesions into categories including Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Nevus. Evaluation results demonstrated high prediction accuracy rates of 98.9%, 98.7%, 98.6%, and 97.9% across the respective datasets. These findings indicate that ACO-KSELM effectively identifies underlying data patterns to improve diagnostic accuracy.

It reports prediction accuracy metrics (98.9%, 98.7%, 98.6%, 97.9%) on four datasets (ISIC 2016, ACS, HAM10000, PAD-UFES-20), which is characteristic of a classification task.

conclusions: The proposed Anti Coronavirus Optimized Kernel-based Softplus Extreme Learning Machine (ACO-KSELM) effectively addresses the challenge of redundant and irrelevant features in high-dimensional biomedical datasets by identifying underlying data patterns.

The ACO-KSELM method achieved high prediction accuracy rates of 98.9%, 98.7%, 98.6%, and 97.9% on the ISIC 2016, ACS, HAM10000, and PAD-UFES-20 datasets, respectively, demonstrating its capability to accurately classify skin cancer types including Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Bowen’s disease, Melanoma, and Nevus.

The study confirms that preprocessing dermoscopic images with Gaussian filters and extracting features based on color, texture, and shape (using color histogram, Haralick texture, and Hu moments) contributes to the high diagnostic accuracy of the proposed model.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on prediction accuracy as the evaluation metric, lacking comprehensive statistical validation such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or confidence intervals, which limits the assessment of clinical robustness.

The paper does not report dataset sample sizes, class distribution balances, or specific train/validation/test splits, raising concerns about potential data leakage or overfitting given the high reported accuracy rates (97.9%–98.9%).

The model utilizes traditional machine learning features (color histogram, Haralick texture, Hu moments) rather than deep learning representations, which may limit its ability to capture complex hierarchical patterns compared to modern CNN-based approaches.

The optimization algorithm 'Anti Coronavirus Optimization' is a novel, non-standard heuristic method; its computational efficiency and scalability compared to established optimizers (e.g., PSO, GA) are not discussed.

No external validation on independent, unseen datasets was performed; all evaluations were conducted on four specific public datasets, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other clinical populations or imaging devices.
